volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Braggart Soldier (Section 1.1.1 - Section 2.1.105)

translation.label: Scaife English translation
translation.citation: Miles Gloriosus, or The Braggart Captain

sections.label: Section 1.1.1
sections.original: Curate ut splendor meo sit clupeo clarior
sections.translation: Take ye care that the lustre of my shield is more bright than the rays of the sun are wont to be at the time when the sky is clear ; that when occasion comes , the battle being joined , ’ mid the fierce ranks right opposite it may dazzle the eyesight of the enemy .

sections.label: Section 1.1.5
sections.original: nam ego hanc machaeram mihi consolari volo ,
sections.translation: But , I wish to console this sabre of mine , that it may not lament nor be downcast in spirits , because I have thus long been wearing it keeping holiday , which so longs right dreadfully to make havoc of the enemy . But where is Artotrogus ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.9b
sections.original: Stat propter virum
sections.translation: Here he is ; he stands close by the hero ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.10
sections.original: fortem atque fortunatum et forma regia ;
sections.translation: valiant and successful , and of princely form . Mars could not dare to style himself a warrior so great , nor compare his prowess with yours .

sections.label: Section 1.1.13
sections.original: Quemne ego servavi in campis Curculioniis ,
sections.translation: Him you mean whom I spared on the Gorgonidonian [*] plains , where Bumbomachides Clytomestoridysarchides ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.15
sections.original: erat imperator summus , Neptuni nepos ?
sections.translation: the grandson of Neptune , was the chief commander ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.16
sections.original: Memini . nempe illum dicis cum armis aureis ,
sections.translation: I remember him ; him , I suppose , you mean with the golden armour , whose legions you puffed away with your breath just as the wind blows away leaves or the reed-thatched roof .

sections.label: Section 1.1.19
sections.original: Istuc quidem edepol nihil est .
sections.translation: That , on my troth , was really nothing at all .

sections.label: Section 1.1.19b
sections.original: Nihil hercle hoc quidemst
sections.translation: Faith , that really was nothing at all

sections.label: Section 1.1.20
sections.original: praeut alia dicam — quae tu numquam feceris .
sections.translation: in comparison with other things I could mention — ( aside ) which you never did . If any person ever beheld a more perjured fellow than this , or one more full of vain boasting , faith let him have me for himself , I ’ ll resign myself for his slave ; if ’ tis not the fact that [*] my one mess of olive pottage [*] is eaten up by me right ravenously .

sections.label: Section 1.1.25
sections.original: Vbi tu es ?
sections.translation: Where are you ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.25b
sections.original: Eccum . edepol vel elephanto in India ,
sections.translation: Lo ! here am I . I ’ troth in what a fashion it was you broke the fore-leg [*] of even an elephant , in India , with your fist .

sections.label: Section 1.1.27
sections.original: Quid , bracchium ?
sections.translation: How ?— the fore-leg ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.27b
sections.original: Illud dicere volui , femur .
sections.translation: I meant to say this — the thigh .

sections.label: Section 1.1.28
sections.original: At indíligenter iceram .
sections.translation: I struck the blow without an effort .

sections.label: Section 1.1.28b
sections.original: Pol si quidem
sections.translation: Troth , if , indeed ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.29
sections.original: conisus esses , per corium , per viscera
sections.translation: you had put forth your strength , your arm would have passed right through the hide , the entrails , and the frontispiece of the elephant .

sections.label: Section 1.1.31
sections.original: Nolo istaec hic nunc .
sections.translation: I don ’ t care for these things just now .

sections.label: Section 1.1.31b
sections.original: Ne hercle operae pretium quidemst
sections.translation: I ’ faith , ’ tis really not worth the while for you to tell me of it , who know right well your prowess . ( Aside ) ’ Tis my appetite creates [*] all these plagues . I must hear him right out with my ears , that my teeth mayn ’ t have time [*] to grow ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.35
sections.original: et adsentandumst quidquid hic mentibitur .
sections.translation: and whatever lie he shall tell , to it I must agree .

sections.label: Section 1.1.36
sections.original: Quid illúc quod dico ?
sections.translation: What was it I was saying ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.36b
sections.original: Ehem , scío iam quid vis dicere .
sections.translation: O , I know what you were going to say just now . I ’ faith ’ twas bravely done ; I remember its being done .

sections.label: Section 1.1.37b
sections.original: Quid id est ?
sections.translation: What was that ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.37c
sections.original: Quidquid est .
sections.translation: Whatever it was you were going to say .

sections.label: Section 1.1.38
sections.original: Habes —
sections.translation: Have you got your tablets [*] ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.38b
sections.original: Tabellas vis rogare ? habeo , et stilum .
sections.translation: Are you intending to enlist [*] ? I have them , and a pen as well .

sections.label: Section 1.1.39
sections.original: Facete advortis tuom animum ad animum meum .
sections.translation: How cleverly you do suit your mind to my own mind .

sections.label: Section 1.1.40
sections.original: Novisse mores tuos me meditate decet
sections.translation: ’ Tis fit that I should know your inclinations studiously , so that whatever you wish should first occur [*] to me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.42
sections.original: Ecquid meministi ?
sections.translation: What do you remember ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.42b
sections.original: Memini : centum in Cilicia
sections.translation: I do remember this . In Cilicia there were a hundred and fifty men , a hundred in Cryphiolathronia [*] , thirty at Sardis , sixty men of Macedon ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.45
sections.original: † sunt homines quos tu occidisti uno die .
sections.translation: whom you slaughtered altogether in one day .

sections.label: Section 1.1.46
sections.original: Quanta istaec hominum summast ?
sections.translation: What is the sum total of those men ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.46b
sections.original: Septem milia .
sections.translation: Seven thousand .

sections.label: Section 1.1.47
sections.original: Tantum esse oportet . recte rationem tenes .
sections.translation: It must be as much : you keep the reckoning well .

sections.label: Section 1.1.48
sections.original: At nullos habeo scriptos : sic memini tamen .
sections.translation: Yet I have none of them written down ; still , so I remember it was .

sections.label: Section 1.1.49
sections.original: Edepol memoria es optuma .
sections.translation: By my troth , you have a right good memory .

sections.label: Section 1.1.49b
sections.original: Offae monent .
sections.translation: ’ Tis the flesh-pots [*] give it a fillip .

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: Dum tale facies quale adhuc , assiduo edes ,
sections.translation: So long as you shall do such as you have done hitherto , you shall always have something to eat : I will always make you a partaker at my table .

sections.label: Section 1.1.52
sections.original: Quid ín Cappadocia , ubi tu quingentos simul ,
sections.translation: Besides , in Cappadocia , you would have killed five hundred men altogether at one blow , had not your sabre been blunt .

sections.label: Section 1.1.54
sections.original: At peditastelli quia erant , sivi viverent .
sections.translation: I let them live , because I was quite sick of fighting .

sections.label: Section 1.1.55
sections.original: Quid tibi ego dicam , quód omnes mortales sciunt ,
sections.translation: Why should I tell you what all mortals know , that you , Pyrgopolinices , live alone upon the earth , with valour , beauty , and achievements most unsurpassed ? All the women are in love with you , and that not without reason , since you are so handsome . Witness those girls that pulled me by my mantle yesterday .

sections.label: Section 1.1.60b
sections.original: Quid eae dixerunt tibi ?
sections.translation: What was it they said to you ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.61
sections.original: Rogitabant : hicine Achilles est ? inquit mihi .
sections.translation: They questioned me about you . Is Achilles here ? says one to me . No , says I , his brother is . Then says the other to me : By my troth , but he is a handsome and a noble man . See how his long hair becomes him .

sections.label: Section 1.1.65
sections.original: ne illae sunt fortunatae quae cum isto cubant .”
sections.translation: Certainly the women are lucky who share his favours .

sections.label: Section 1.1.66
sections.original: Itane aibant tandem ?
sections.translation: And pray , did they really say so ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.66b
sections.original: Quaen me ambae obsecraverint ,
sections.translation: They both entreated me to bring you past to-day by way of a sight [*] to them .

sections.label: Section 1.1.68
sections.original: Nimiast miseria nimis pulchrum esse hominem .
sections.translation: ’ Tis really a very great plague to be too handsome a man .

sections.label: Section 1.1.68b
sections.original: Immo itast .
sections.translation: They are quite a nuisance to me ; they are praying , entreating , beseeching me ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.70
sections.original: videre ut liceat , ad sese arcessi iubent ,
sections.translation: to let them see you ; bidding me be fetched to them ; so that I can ’ t give my attention to your business .

sections.label: Section 1.1.72
sections.original: Videtur tempus esse , ut eamus ad forum ,
sections.translation: It seems that it is time for us to go to the Forum , that I may count out their pay to those soldiers whom I have enlisted of late .

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.original: nam rex Seleucus me opere oravit maxumo ,
sections.translation: For King Seleucus [*] entreated me with most earnest suit that I would raise and enlist recruits for him . To that business have I resolved to devote my attention this day .

sections.label: Section 1.1.78
sections.original: Age eamus ergo .
sections.translation: Come , let ’ s be going then .

sections.label: Section 1.1.78b
sections.original: Sequimini , satellites . —
sections.translation: Guards , follow me .

sections.label: Section 2.1.79
sections.original: Mihi ad enarrandum hoc argumentum est comitas ,
sections.translation: To tell the subject of this our play , I have all willingness ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.80
sections.original: si ad auscultandum vostra erit benignitas ;
sections.translation: if you will but have the kindness to listen to it . But he who does not wish to listen , let him arise and go out , that there may be room where he may sit who does wish to listen . Now I will disclose to you both the subject and the name of the play which we are just now about to act , and for the sake of which you are now seated in this mirthful place [*] ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.86
sections.original: Alazon Graece huic nomen est comoediae ,
sections.translation: Alazon is the name [*] , in Greek , of this Comedy ; the same we call in Latin , the Braggart ( Gloriosus ). This city is Ephesus ; then , the Captain , my master , who has gone off hence to the Forum , a bragging , impudent ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.90
sections.original: stercoreus , plenus periuri atque adulteri .
sections.translation: stinking fellow , brimful of lying and lasciviousness , says that all the women are following him of their own accord . Wherever he goes , he is the laughing . stock of all ; and so , the Courtesans here — since they make wry mouths at him , you may see the greater part of them with lips all awry .

sections.label: Section 2.1.95
sections.original: nam ego hau diu apud hunc servitutem servio ;
sections.translation: I wish you now to know this , how I came to be his slave , from him to whom I was servant before ; for ’ tis not long that I have been in slavery to him . Give your attention , for now I will begin the argument . A very worthy young man at Athens was my master .

sections.label: Section 2.1.100
sections.original: is amabat meretricem ⋯ matre Athenis Atticis ,
sections.translation: He was in love with a Courtesan , brought up at Athens , in Attica , and she on the other hand loved him ; such affection is most worthy to be cherished . In the public service , he was sent to Naupactus [*] as Ambassador on behalf of that mighty republic . In the mean time , by chance , this Captain came to Athens .

sections.label: Section 2.1.105
sections.original: insinuat sese ad illam amicam eri †
sections.translation: He introduced himself to this lady of my master , began to cajole her mother with presents of wine , trinkets , and costly treats ; and so the Captain made himself on intimate terms with the procuress . As soon as ever an opportunity was presented for this Captain ,
